response: Electric vehicles trace their roots to 1830s prototypes, but mass-market momentum only arrived after Tesla’s 2012-era breakthroughs proved that long-range, high-performance EVs could be commercially viable. Progress is still gated by battery cost, lithium- and cobalt-dependent supply chains, patchy charging infrastructure, and consumer worries about range and fast-charging times, even as utilities study whether today’s grids can meet the added load. Spurred by billions in U.S. and European purchase subsidies and mandates, legacy giants such as Ford, GM, and VW have pledged to electrify most of their line-ups this decade, while China—now the world’s largest EV producer and market—leverages state support and domestic mineral control to dominate global sales. Solid-state batteries, which promise higher energy density and shorter charging, are viewed as the next leap that could resolve many of these technical and environmental challenges.
